result.markdown:
--- title: "Forward pricer — a formula graph from scratch" subtitle: "Headless formula parameters, semantic market roles, one lazy Polars plan" --- Schenberg is a **lazy pricing DSL**. A pricer is a small *symbolic* program — a `FormulaGraph` of named terms — and the same program compiles three ways from one declaration: - to a lazy **Polars** expression (what runs), - to **LaTeX** derived from the formula itself (what you read), and - to a **Mermaid** dependency graph (what you reason about). Market data never enters the graph. It is resolved into ordinary input columns by `bind(...)` *before* the graph runs, so the graph stays a pure function of its input frame. ::: {#18506f8e .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Imports & display helpers"} from datetime import date import polars as pl from schenberg import ( FormulaGraph, MarketSnapshot, With, bind, exp, market_role, ) from schenberg.domain.base import SchenbergDataFrameModel from schenberg_quarto import show_formulas, show_graph, show_explain ``` ::: ## 1. Declare market data as *roles*, outside the graph A `market_role` says *where* a column comes from and *how* to join it — never how to price. Here a forward rate and a risk-free rate are read from a `curves` source, keyed by indexer and tenor. ::: {#2b4d4687 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} ForwardRate = ( market_role("forward_rate") .read("curves", "forward_rate") .by(indexer="id_indexador", payment_days="tenor_days") ) RiskFreeRate = ( market_role("risk_free_rate") .read("curves", "risk_free_rate") .by(indexer="id_indexador", payment_days="tenor_days") ) class ForwardInput(With[ForwardRate], With[RiskFreeRate], SchenbergDataFrameModel): instrument_id: str indexer: str currency: str strike: float payment_days: int ``` ::: ## 2. Write the math as headless formulas Each formula parameter is a **headless dependency**: a bare name resolved to a prior term or an input column. The function body reads like the math — no `c.`/`contract.` indirection. ::: {#44f9c9e3 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} g = FormulaGraph("forward", input=ForwardInput) @g.formula(symbol="T", description="business-day year fraction") def year_fraction(payment_days): return payment_days / 252.0 @g.formula(symbol="DF", description="discount factor") def discount_factor(risk_free_rate, year_fraction): return exp(-risk_free_rate * year_fraction) @g.formula(symbol="FV", description="forward minus strike") def future_value(forward_rate, strike): return forward_rate - strike @g.formula(symbol="PV", description="present value") def present_value(future_value, discount_factor): return future_value * discount_factor @g.formula(symbol="\\Delta") def delta(discount_factor): return discount_factor g.returns( "output", instrument_id="instrument_id", future_value="future_value", present_value="present_value", value="present_value", delta="delta", currency="currency", ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` ``` ::: ::: ## 3. The graph renders itself Because terms are symbolic, the LaTeX below is *derived from the IR* — it can never drift from what executes. ``` {.python .cell-code} show_formulas(g) ``` **Formulas** $$ T = \frac{\mathrm{payment\_days}}{252.0} $$ $$ DF = e^{-\mathrm{risk\_free\_rate} \cdot \mathrm{year\_fraction}} $$ $$ FV = \mathrm{forward\_rate} - \mathrm{strike} $$ $$ PV = \mathrm{future\_value} \cdot \mathrm{discount\_factor} $$ $$ \Delta = \mathrm{discount\_factor} $$ And the dependency DAG, straight from `graph.to_mermaid()`: ``` {.python .cell-code} show_graph(g) ``` ```{mermaid} flowchart LR payment_days --> year_fraction risk_free_rate --> discount_factor year_fraction --> discount_factor forward_rate --> future_value strike --> future_value future_value --> present_value discount_factor --> present_value discount_factor --> delta ``` ``` {.python .cell-code} show_explain(g, view="output") ``` ```text FormulaGraph forward View: - output Inputs: currency, forward_rate, instrument_id, payment_days, risk_free_rate, strike Formulas: - T = \frac{\mathrm{payment\_days}}{252.0} # business-day year fraction - DF = e^{-\mathrm{risk\_free\_rate} \cdot \mathrm{year\_fraction}} # discount factor - FV = \mathrm{forward\_rate} - \mathrm{strike} # forward minus strike - PV = \mathrm{future\_value} \cdot \mathrm{discount\_factor} # present value - \Delta = \mathrm{discount\_factor} Returns: - instrument_id <- instrument_id - future_value <- future_value - present_value <- present_value - value <- present_value - delta <- delta - currency <- currency ``` ## 4. Bind market data, then plan lazily ::: {#277644b8 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} trades = pl.DataFrame( { "instrument_id": ["FWD-1", "FWD-2"], "indexer": ["DI", "DI"], "currency": ["BRL", "BRL"], "strike": [100.0, 105.0], "payment_days": [252, 504], } ).lazy() market = ( MarketSnapshot.at(date(2026, 6, 6)) .source( "curves", pl.DataFrame( { "id_indexador": ["DI", "DI"], "tenor_days": [252, 504], "forward_rate": [112.0, 118.0], "risk_free_rate": [0.10, 0.11], } ), unique_by=("id_indexador", "tenor_days"), ) .build() ) plan = g.plan(bind(trades, market, ForwardInput), view="output") type(plan).__name__ # still lazy — nothing has been collected ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ``` 'LazyFrame' ``` ::: ::: `bind` resolved the two market roles into `forward_rate` / `risk_free_rate` columns; `plan` returned a `LazyFrame`. We collect only at the very end: ::: {#5f375616 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} plan.collect() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ```{=html}
shape: (2, 11)

``` ::: ::: The whole pricer is one `with_columns` over a `LazyFrame`: no Python loops, no row-wise UDFs, no `.collect()` hidden inside the graph. The next notebooks build Black-Scholes Greeks, autodiff vanna/volga, scenario VaR, and a QuantLib benchmark on this same spine.
